items.name: Database
items.description: The ORM entry point. Connect to a database using one of 7 built-in adapters (memory, json, sqlite, mysql, postgres, mongo, redis), register your Model classes, and sync schemas. The Database instance manages the connection lifecycle and provides transaction support. All network-facing adapters (mysql, postgres, mongo, redis) validate credentials on connect — invalid host, port, user, or database values throw immediately.

options.notes: In-memory adapter. No options required. Great for testing and prototyping.
File-based JSON adapter. Options: { path: 'data.json' }.
SQLite adapter. Options: { filename, readonly, fileMustExist, createDir, pragmas }. Requires better-sqlite3. Auto-creates parent directories.
MySQL adapter. Options: { host, user, password, database, port }. Requires mysql2. Credentials validated on connect.
PostgreSQL adapter. Options: { host, user, password, database, port, ssl }. Requires pg. Credentials validated on connect.
MongoDB adapter. Options: { url, database, clientOptions }. Requires mongodb. URL and database validated on connect.
Redis adapter. Options: { url, host, port, password, db, prefix }. Requires ioredis. Key-value, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, pub/sub.

tips: Use 'memory' for tests and prototyping — zero setup, instant startup.
SQLite with WAL mode handles concurrent reads and writes well for single-server apps.
All SQLite pragmas have production-ready defaults — you only need to set filename.
createDir: true (default) auto-creates parent directories for the database file.
Network adapter credentials (host, port, user, password, database) are type-checked on connect.
db.transaction() wraps your callback in begin/commit/rollback when the adapter supports it.
db.register() returns the Database instance — you can chain: db.register(User).register(Post).
Always call db.sync() after registering models to create the database tables.
FK actions (onDelete, onUpdate) and CHECK expressions are automatically validated against injection — invalid values throw immediately.
Import validateFKAction / validateCheck from 'zero-http' for custom DDL validation outside the ORM.

items.name: Model
items.description: The ORM base class — extend it to define your data models. Supports typed schemas with validation, timestamps, soft deletes, lifecycle hooks, hidden fields, reusable scopes, relationships (hasMany, hasOne, belongsTo, belongsToMany), and a full suite of CRUD operations.

options.notes: Database table name (required).

Column definitions with type, required, default, unique, primaryKey, autoIncrement, enum, min, max, minLength, maxLength, match, guarded, references, check, index, compositeKey, compositeUnique, compositeIndex.
Auto-manage createdAt and updatedAt columns.
Mark records as deleted (deletedAt) instead of removing them. Use .restore() to undelete.
Lifecycle hooks: beforeCreate, afterCreate, beforeUpdate, afterUpdate, beforeDelete, afterDelete.
Fields excluded from toJSON() output. Use for passwords, tokens, internal IDs.
Named reusable query conditions. Each scope is a function: (query, ...args) => query.where(...).

tips: Model.first/last/all/random/pluck are shortcuts — they build queries internally so you don't have to.
Model.paginate() returns metadata (total, pages, hasNext, hasPrev) — perfect for REST API pagination endpoints.
Model.chunk() processes large tables in batches — avoids loading millions of records into memory at once.
static hidden = ['password'] prevents accidental password leaks in API responses.
Scopes are the most powerful feature for DRY queries — define once, reuse everywhere.
upsert() is atomic find-or-create-or-update — avoids race conditions in concurrent environments.
increment/decrement save immediately — no need to call instance.save() after.
guarded: true on a schema field prevents it from being set via mass-assignment (create/update with object).
Hooks are great for data normalization (lowercase emails) and audit logging.
belongsToMany requires a junction table name and the foreign keys for both sides.

items.name: Schema DDL
items.description: Advanced schema options for DDL generation. Define foreign keys, CHECK constraints, indexes, composite primary keys, composite unique constraints, and composite indexes directly in your schema — the ORM generates the correct DDL for every adapter. Sync ordering is automatic: tables with foreign key references are created after their targets.

options.notes: Foreign key: { table, column, onDelete, onUpdate }. Generates REFERENCES clause in SQL. onDelete/onUpdate accept CASCADE, SET NULL, RESTRICT, NO ACTION.
SQL CHECK constraint expression. e.g. '"price" > 0'. Applied inline on the column.
Create a single-column index. true = auto-named, string = custom index name. e.g. index: 'idx_users_email'.
Mark as part of a composite primary key. Set primaryKey: true and compositeKey: true on each column.
Group columns into a composite UNIQUE constraint. Columns with the same string value form one constraint.
Group columns into a composite index. Columns with the same string value form one multi-column index.
Prevent mass-assignment of this field via create/update. Must be set explicitly on the instance.
Column data type (e.g. `TYPES.STRING`, `TYPES.INTEGER`). Required.
Field must be provided on create. Throws validation error if missing.
Default value, or a factory function. Called on each `create()` if the field is omitted.
Allow null values in the column.
Mark this column as the primary key.
Auto-increment on insert (integer primary keys).
Add a UNIQUE constraint on this column.
Minimum string length. Validated before insert/update.
Maximum string length. Validated before insert/update.
Minimum numeric value.
Maximum numeric value.
Pattern constraint for string values.
Allowed values for string/ENUM type.
Allowed values for SET type.
Total digit count for DECIMAL types.
Digits after the decimal point for DECIMAL types.
Fixed width for CHAR, BINARY, VARBINARY types.
MySQL: mark column as unsigned (no negative values).
MySQL/PG: column-level character set.
MySQL/PG: column-level collation.
MySQL/PG: column comment stored in database metadata.
PostgreSQL: element type for ARRAY columns (e.g. `"text"`, `"integer"`).

tips: references generates real FK constraints in SQL — enforced at the database level, not just app level.
db.sync() automatically creates referenced tables first (topological ordering).
compositeKey: true creates a multi-column PRIMARY KEY — perfect for junction tables.
compositeUnique groups columns into a single UNIQUE constraint — e.g. (userId, role) must be unique together.
compositeIndex groups columns into a single multi-column index — speeds up queries filtering on both columns.
check constraints are raw SQL expressions — use double-quoted column names for portability.
Migration methods (addColumn, dropColumn, renameColumn) work on all adapters including memory.
hasTable/hasColumn are essential before running migrations — check before you change.
describeTable returns adapter-specific column metadata — columns, types, defaults, and PK flags.
The memory adapter enforces unique and compositeUnique constraints at insert time — throws on duplicates.
MongoDB adapter uses JSON Schema validation and unique indexes from schema definitions.

items.name: TYPES
items.description: Column type constants for defining model schemas. Each type maps to the appropriate native type in the target database adapter.

options.notes: Variable-length string (VARCHAR).
Whole number (INT).
Floating-point number (REAL/DOUBLE).
True/false (BOOLEAN/TINYINT).
Date without time.
Date with time (TIMESTAMP).
JSON column (auto-serialize/deserialize). PostgreSQL maps to JSONB.
Large text field (TEXT/CLOB).
Binary data (BLOB/BYTEA).
UUID string. Can auto-generate with default: () => crypto.randomUUID().
64-bit integer (BIGINT).
16-bit integer (SMALLINT).
8-bit integer (TINYINT in MySQL, SMALLINT in PG).
Fixed-precision number. Use precision/scale in schema: { precision: 10, scale: 2 }.
Double-precision float (DOUBLE / DOUBLE PRECISION).
Single-precision float (REAL).
Fixed-length string. Use length in schema: { length: 10 }.
Fixed-length binary. Use length in schema.
Variable-length binary. Use length in schema.
Timestamp with time zone (TIMESTAMPTZ in PG).
Time without date.
MySQL ENUM. Define values: { enum: ['admin', 'user', 'guest'] }.
MySQL SET (multi-value). Define values: { values: ['read', 'write', 'delete'] }.
MySQL medium text (16 MB max).
MySQL long text (4 GB max).
MySQL medium blob (16 MB max).
MySQL long blob (4 GB max).
MySQL YEAR type (4-digit year).
PostgreSQL auto-incrementing integer.
PostgreSQL auto-incrementing 64-bit integer.
PostgreSQL binary JSON (indexable, faster queries).
PostgreSQL time interval.
PostgreSQL IPv4/IPv6 address.
PostgreSQL CIDR network address.
PostgreSQL MAC address.
PostgreSQL money type.
PostgreSQL XML type.
PostgreSQL case-insensitive text (requires extension).
PostgreSQL array. Use arrayOf in schema: { arrayOf: 'TEXT' }.
SQLite NUMERIC affinity. Maps to REAL/INTEGER based on value.

items.example:
const { TYPES } = require('zero-http')

static schema = {
	id:        { type: TYPES.INTEGER, primaryKey: true, autoIncrement: true },
	uuid:      { type: TYPES.UUID, default: () => crypto.randomUUID() },
	name:      { type: TYPES.STRING, required: true, minLength: 2, maxLength: 100 },
	bio:       { type: TYPES.TEXT },
	age:       { type: TYPES.INTEGER, min: 0, max: 150 },
	score:     { type: TYPES.FLOAT, default: 0.0 },
	active:    { type: TYPES.BOOLEAN, default: true },
	birthday:  { type: TYPES.DATE },
	loginAt:   { type: TYPES.DATETIME },
	settings:  { type: TYPES.JSON, default: {} },
	avatar:    { type: TYPES.BLOB },
	price:     { type: TYPES.DECIMAL, precision: 10, scale: 2 },
	role:      { type: TYPES.ENUM, enum: ['admin', 'user', 'guest'] },
	perms:     { type: TYPES.SET, values: ['read', 'write', 'delete'] },
	tags:      { type: TYPES.ARRAY, arrayOf: 'TEXT' },
	ip:        { type: TYPES.INET },
	code:      { type: TYPES.CHAR, length: 6 }
}

items.name: Query

items.name: SQLite Adapter
items.description: The SQLite adapter uses better-sqlite3 for synchronous, high-performance file-based persistence. It auto-creates parent directories, ships with production-tuned PRAGMA defaults (WAL, 64 MB cache, memory-mapped I/O), and exposes utility methods for database maintenance. Ideal for single-server apps, prototyping, and embedded use cases.

options.notes: Path to the SQLite file, or ':memory:' for an in-memory database.
Open the database in read-only mode.
Throw if the database file does not exist.
Auto-create parent directories for the database file.
Log all executed SQL statements to the console.
SQLite PRAGMA overrides. All keys below are set inside this object.
WAL mode enables concurrent reads/writes. Options: WAL, DELETE, TRUNCATE, MEMORY, OFF.
Enforce foreign key constraints.
Milliseconds to wait when the database is locked.
Sync mode: OFF, NORMAL, FULL, EXTRA.
Page cache size. Negative = KiB (e.g. -64000 = 64 MB).
Store temp tables in memory for speed.
Memory-mapped I/O size (256 MB).
Page size in bytes. Must be set before WAL is enabled.
Auto-vacuum mode: NONE, FULL, INCREMENTAL.
Overwrite deleted content with zeros.
Number of pages before WAL auto-checkpoint (default 1000).
NORMAL or EXCLUSIVE locking.

tips: WAL mode (default) gives you concurrent reads while a single writer commits — ideal for web servers.
cache_size of -64000 means 64 MB of page cache — enough for most applications.
mmap_size of 256 MB uses memory-mapped I/O for fast reads on large databases.
Use checkpoint('TRUNCATE') periodically to reset the WAL file size.
integrity() is useful in health-check endpoints to verify database consistency.
readonly mode is perfect for read replicas — prevents accidental writes.
The adapter auto-creates parent directories — just point filename to 'Database/app.db'.
overview() is a one-call dashboard — returns tables, row counts, total rows, and formatted file size.
columns(table) + indexes(table) + foreignKeys(table) give you full schema introspection.
cacheStatus() shows how many prepared statements are cached — helps tune stmtCacheSize.
pageInfo() reveals the page size and count — useful for diagnosing storage overhead.

items.name: MySQL Adapter
items.description: MySQL / MariaDB adapter using the mysql2 driver with connection pooling, prepared statements, and utility methods for introspection and maintenance. Supports SSL, custom charsets, timezone configuration, pool health monitoring, and built-in debug methods for table status, indexes, foreign keys, and server variables.

Database name (required). Validated as non-empty string.

Default character set (utf8mb4 for full emoji support).

tips: Always use environment variables for credentials — never hardcode passwords!
connectionLimit of 10-20 is a safe default. Go higher only if you're seeing pool exhaustion.
poolStatus() is perfect for monitoring dashboards — track idle vs used connections.
Use charset: 'utf8mb4' (default) to support emojis and full Unicode.
ping() is ideal for health check endpoints in load balancers.
exec() is for writes that don't return rows — raw() is for SELECTs.

tableStatus() + tableSize() + indexes() give you a full database inspector.
overview() returns all tables with sizes and row counts — one call for a dashboard view.
variables('innodb%') filters server variables by pattern — great for tuning diagnostics.
processlist() shows active queries — use it to spot slow queries and stuck connections.
alterTable() lets you change engine/charset/collation live — e.g. migrate from MyISAM to InnoDB.
createTable supports tableOptions: { engine, charset, collation, comment } for full control.

processlist() shows active backends — use it to spot long-running queries in production.

createTable supports tableOptions: { tablespace, unlogged, comment } and column-level references.

items.name: Redis Adapter
items.description: Redis adapter using ioredis with key-value operations, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, pub/sub, pipelines, TTL management, and full ORM CRUD. Stores table data as Redis hashes with sorted-set indexes for ordering and filtering. Bring-your-own-driver: npm install ioredis.

Redis database index. Must be an integer between 0 and 15.

Defer connection until first operation.

tips: Redis adapter requires the ioredis package — install with: npm install ioredis.
Use prefix to namespace keys — avoids collisions when sharing a Redis instance.
set() with a TTL is perfect for sessions, rate limiting, and temporary data.
Pipeline batches multiple commands into a single round-trip — much faster than individual calls.
Pub/Sub uses a separate connection internally — subscribing won't block your main client.
The ORM CRUD layer (Model.create, find, etc.) works on top of Redis hashes + sorted sets.
Use url for hosted Redis (Upstash, Redis Cloud, AWS ElastiCache).

items.name: Migrator
items.description: Versioned migration framework for the ORM. Define up/down migrations, track execution in batches, rollback safely, check status, and perform fresh resets. Migrations are stored in a _migrations tracking table within the same database.

Each migrate() call creates a new batch — rollback() undoes the last batch only.
rollbackAll() reverses all batches — useful for tearing down test databases.
fresh() is destructive — it drops ALL tables including non-migrated ones.
status() shows which migrations have run and which are pending — great for deploy checks.
hasPending() is perfect for startup checks — block the server until migrations are current.
The _migrations tracking table is auto-created on first use.

items.name: QueryCache
items.description: In-memory LRU query cache with TTL support. Attach to a Database instance to cache query results automatically. Supports manual get/set, model-level invalidation, remember pattern, and optional Redis backend for distributed caching.

tips: LRU eviction removes the least-recently-used entry when maxEntries is reached.
remember() is the most common pattern — avoids cache stampedes with a single call.

stats() returns hitRate as a 0–1 float — multiply by 100 for a percentage.
prune() removes expired entries proactively — useful for memory-constrained environments.
Pass a Redis adapter instance as options.redis for distributed caching across processes.
flush() also resets hit/miss counters — useful for testing.

items.name: QueryProfiler
items.description: Automatic query profiling, slow-query detection, and N+1 pattern identification. Attach to any Database instance via db.enableProfiling(). Records every query execution with timing, flags queries exceeding a configurable threshold, and detects rapid repeated SELECTs on the same table within a time window. Capped history prevents memory leaks in long-running servers.

options.notes: Enable/disable profiling.
Duration in ms above which a query is flagged as slow.
Maximum number of query entries retained in memory.
Callback invoked on every slow query: (entry) => {}.
Minimum rapid same-table SELECTs to flag as N+1.
Time window in ms for N+1 detection.
Callback invoked on N+1 detection: (info) => {}.
Maximum N+1 detection entries to retain. Oldest entries are evicted when exceeded.

tips: enableProfiling() returns the profiler — store it or access later via db.profiler.
slowThreshold defaults to 100 ms — lower it during development, raise it in production.
maxHistory caps query retention to prevent memory growth in long-running processes.
onSlow and onN1 callbacks let you pipe alerts to external monitoring (Sentry, Datadog, etc.).
N+1 detection watches for repeated SELECTs on the same table within n1Window ms — tune n1Threshold for your workload.
Use profiler.reset() between test runs to isolate measurements.
maxN1History caps N+1 detection entries — prevents unbounded memory growth from recurring patterns.

items.name: ReplicaManager
items.description: Read replica load balancing for horizontal read scaling. Distributes read queries across replica database connections using round-robin or random strategies. Supports health checks, sticky writes (reads go to primary briefly after a write to avoid stale data), and automatic fallback to primary when all replicas are down.

methods.description: Set the primary (read-write) adapter. Throws if adapter is null.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methods.description: Get the next read adapter. Returns primary during sticky window after a write, otherwise selects from healthy replicas using the configured strategy. Falls back to primary if no healthy replicas.

methodReturns.description: Selected replica adapter using the configured selection strategy (round-robin or random).

methods.description: Get the primary adapter for write operations. Records the write timestamp for sticky-write tracking.

options.notes: Replica selection strategy. Must be 'round-robin' or 'random'. Throws on invalid value.
When true, reads go to primary for a brief window after a write to avoid stale data.
Duration in ms to read from primary after a write (sticky-write window).

tips: connectWithReplicas() is the easiest way to set up replicas — it wires everything automatically.
Sticky writes prevent reading stale data right after a write — keep stickyWindow short (1-2s).
healthCheck() pings every replica — run it on an interval in production for automatic failover.
Use onReplica() in queries to explicitly route a specific query to a read replica.
strategy must be 'round-robin' or 'random' — invalid values throw immediately.
If all replicas are unhealthy, reads automatically fall back to the primary.
